spec: Rank the provided cat pictures by their immediacy of emotional impact — the speed and involuntariness of the feeling each image provokes upon first viewing. A picture with high immediacy hits the viewer before they have time to think about it: a reflexive laugh, a sudden gasp, an involuntary softening of the face. The emotional content is front and center, seizing attention the instant the image registers — no study, no context, no interpretation required. A cat frozen mid-fall with a look of pure bewilderment, or a tiny kitten dwarfed inside an oversized shoe, lands immediately and lands hard. Evaluate purely the gut-punch moment — the first half-second of contact — independent of whether the feeling persists or deepens afterward. Images where the emotional element is buried, subtle, or dependent on prolonged viewing should rank lower. Images that bypass thought entirely and arrive as pure reflexive reaction should rank highest. Technical quality, composition, and aesthetic polish are irrelevant — only the speed and force of the initial emotional hit matters.

Rank the provided cat pictures by the depth of feeling each one provokes — the weight, staying power, and emotional penetration of the response beyond the initial moment of contact. An image with high depth does not just make the viewer react; it makes them stay, linger, return, and remember. It captures something that gestures toward larger human experiences — tenderness, vulnerability, innocence, companionship, aging, wonder — in a way that settles beneath the surface and resurfaces unbidden later. An elderly cat resting peacefully in fading light, a cat gently grooming a companion who is clearly unwell, a kitten encountering snow for the first time with its whole body a question mark of confusion and wonder — these images tap into something that outlasts the act of looking. Evaluate how deeply the image penetrates beneath the surface of a quick reaction and how likely the feeling is to endure. Not just whether the viewer reacts, but whether they remember. Images that provoke only fleeting surface reactions should rank lower. Images that carry emotional weight the viewer takes with them should rank highest. Technical quality, composition, and aesthetic polish are irrelevant — only the depth and durability of the feeling matters.

Rank the provided cat pictures by the strength of the urge to share each one creates — the compulsion a viewer feels to immediately show the image to someone else. An image that scores highly on this dimension carries an emotional charge so potent that experiencing it alone feels incomplete. It makes a person reach for their phone to send it to a friend, or nudge a companion and point at the screen, almost before they have finished processing their own reaction. This is not about broad generic appeal but about that specific, almost involuntary impulse to pass the feeling along — the confidence that this image will not just move one particular viewer but will reliably land for nearly anyone who sees it. The urge to share captures the universality and transferability of the emotional response. An image might provoke a mild personal reaction without ever crossing the threshold where you would bother sharing it; such images should rank lower. An image might be deeply personal in its emotional content but too idiosyncratic to land for others; it too should rank lower on this dimension. The images that rank highest are those too good to keep to oneself — the ones that make sharing feel not like a choice but like a reflex. Technical quality, composition, and aesthetic polish are irrelevant — only the strength of the compulsion to show someone else matters.
